body:
Outside the door to the master bedroom at Hawthorne Estate, two maids were gossiping loudly, their mocking words slipping through the crack and stabbing into Evelyn Sinclair's ears.

"General Hawthorne already has the divorce papers ready, and she's still hiding in there, pretending to be dead," one of them snickered.

"Seriously," the other scoffed. "She had the nerve to drug him. Now she's suddenly too ashamed to show her face, huh?"

"We all thought she was the Sinclair family heiress, but turns out she's just a fake. I can't believe she's still dreaming she could latch onto our general," the first maid added with a sneer.

"Just wait. Once her family receives those papers, she'll be thrown out of this estate for good," the second said.

Inside the room, a round pearwood stool lay toppled on the floor, and a length of white silk was scattered in a tangled mess.

The copper dressing mirror reflected a haggard face. Evelyn's arched brows were slightly furrowed, lending her a fragile, pitiful look.

Her nose was delicate, and her lips, once as rosy as spring blossoms, were now pale and bloodless. Even with features as fine as a painting, nothing could hide her disheveled and drained appearance.

Evelyn's fingers brushed the red marks left by the silk around her neck. Her throat burned.

She thought, 'Who could have imagined that I, Princess Aurora, the Princess Royal of the Aethelgard Kingdom, who once held power over the realm and had admirers flocking to me every whim, would ever fall so low?'

It all started when someone had reported to Evelyn that a character in a popular folk story shared her name. Curious, she'd had the book brought to her. To her shock, the villainess in the story was also named Evelyn Sinclair.

The story was set in a made-up kingdom. As the legitimate daughter of Richard Sinclair, Marquis of Peanolie, Evelyn had been spoiled rotten since childhood.

She'd grown up entitled and arrogant, never bothering to read or learn a thing. The whole city of Colelinton secretly mocked her as an idiot.

But two years ago, Evelyn had stumbled upon a devastating secret: sixteen years earlier, the family's butler, seeking revenge, had bribed the midwife into swapping the heiress with an abandoned baby he had randomly picked from the streets.

As a result, the fake, Evelyn, was showered with love and raised in luxury, while the real heiress was forced to serve as one of the lowest maids in her own home.

Once she found out the truth, the Evelyn in the story had promptly poisoned the butler to silence him. Then she'd dragged the real heiress to be her personal maid, tormenting her daily.

Meanwhile, terrified of being exposed and thrown out of Peanolie Manor, she'd schemed to find a powerful protector.

She'd set her sights on the famous and notoriously cold general, Gavin Hawthorne. She had someone drug his drink, staged a scene to play the victim, and left him with no choice but to marry her.

But the day after the wedding, the midwife came forward and exposed her as a fake. Gavin and his family also learned about the whole drugging thing. The Hawthorne family wanted her gone, and the Sinclair family would not take her back.

In the story, with nowhere left to turn, Evelyn had hanged herself from the rafters. No one claimed her body. She was dumped in a mass grave.

After Evelyn's death, the true heiress was welcomed back to the family, cherished like a rare jewel. She soon captivated the entire Colelinton.

The cold general softened only for her. Her obsessive younger brother showered her with a massive inheritance. The heir to a duke galloped through fields of flowers just to make her laugh.

Even the aloof prime minister, who rarely showed interest in anyone, would gently brush the snow from her shoulders.

In the end, the true heiress was adorned in the full splendor of a royal bride and married the crown prince. Then, as he ascended the throne, she became queen and lived out a legendary love story with him.

No one remembered the forgotten ghost in the mass grave. Anyone who did would only spit in contempt.

As the Princess Royal, Evelyn had been her younger brother's favorite, indulging in every luxury and pleasure. Countless commoners secretly resented her, wishing for her downfall.

Evelyn knew clearly that the story had been written by some bitter scholar who couldn't stand her, using it as a thinly veiled jab at her.

He had twisted her into a stupid, vicious woman, wishing the same miserable fate upon her. Meanwhile, the heroine, crafted merely as a contrast, had been written as heaven's blessed darling.

Evelyn was about to order the author found and executed when the world spun. The next thing she knew, she was inside the story herself. If she hadn't snapped out of it in time, she'd have hanged herself just like the Evelyn in the book.

She thought, 'Well, since I'm here now and have become that girl, it's fine. They could call me vicious. But stupid? Not a chance. Even in this wretched state, I wouldn't let myself die with my body unclaimed.'

Evelyn sat at the dressing table and stared into the mirror.

Back at Aurora Manor, her daily routine was lavish: a pearl-infused tonic at dawn, a portion of the rarest black caviar in the afternoon, and each evening, a milk-and-rosewater bath followed by a mask of jasmine and crushed pearls.

Though it paled next to the breathtaking beauty she once possessed, the face in the mirror held its own allure: fine, well-shaped brows, eyes that held a quiet depth, and skin of such youthful smoothness it betrayed a life of sheltered comfort.

Evelyn idly opened the makeup box. Inside was a single eyebrow pencil with a plain handle, its sable-hair brush sparse and dull from poor crafting. It was nothing of quality.

'Some general's estate, huh? This cheap thing isn't even worth using,' Evelyn thought. 'But whatever. No point making things harder for myself right now.'

She remembered her old life. Getting ready had been an event. Attendants would stand by with her jeweled makeup case, sit on their heels to smooth out her hair with a sandalwood comb, and hold censers to perfume her locks.

Also, a lady-in-waiting skilled in the arts would carefully blend eyebrow powder imported from Westinford.

All Evelyn had to do was lounge on her chaise, hearing the musicians play, while sipping iced cordial.

Now, the reflection showed only her, all alone. She had to do everything herself. Luckily, she had a knack for painting. She'd never done her own brows before, but she'd seen it done enough times.

*****

A quarter of an hour later, Evelyn pushed the door open. Sunlight from this new world poured over her, wrapping her in a golden glow.

Sophie, the maid waiting outside, jerked her head up. When she saw Evelyn, she instinctively stepped back, stammering, "Y-you..."

'Is this the same person from this morning?' Sophie thought, eyes wide in disbelief.

Gone was the ashen, defeated look. Now, Evelyn's brows were sharply defined, a tiny crimson beauty mark graced the corner of her eye, her lips were the color of ripe berries, and her cheeks glowed with a soft, rosy blush.

Evelyn casually smoothed her wind-tossed hair and gave Sophie a cool, indifferent glance.

"I hear General Hawthorne is returning soon," she said, looking up. "Do me a favor. Let him know I'd like to see him before he throws me out."

Sophie clearly didn't want to, but she had no choice. Evelyn was still the lady of the house, for now. "Fine," Sophie muttered through gritted teeth.

Once Sophie was out of sight, Evelyn strolled back into her bedroom.

A half-finished bowl of cold sweet soup sat on the low table. She wrinkled her nose and turned to the gilded cabinet.

In a hidden compartment at the bottom, she found a stash of snacks—some dried fruit, a few pastries, and a small packet of nuts. They weren't the royal-grade treats she was used to, but they'd fill her stomach.

She picked up a pastry and took a bite. It tasted terrible, but she had plans that required energy. She couldn't do it on an empty stomach.

Grimacing, she swallowed it down. 'Guess the sages had a point. You have to endure a little bitterness if you want to come out on top,' she thought.

About fifteen minutes later, the door swung open. A tall man filled the doorway, his shadow stretching into the room. He looked at Evelyn, his voice as cold and devoid of warmth as ice.

"You wanted to see me? What for?" Gavin said.

..............................................................................
Gavin stood in the doorway. When Evelyn looked up, her eyes met his icy stare.

She sized him up instinctively. He was tall and broad-shouldered, with a trim waist. Sunlight caught him from the side, frosting the sharp angles of his face.

A scar under his left brow added a ruthless, battle-hardened edge to his good looks, making him seem carved from stone.

Evelyn arched a brow. She'd seen countless handsome men from the southern regions and kept plenty of lovers. But none had radiated such a dangerous, untamed aura. The tense line of his jaw reminded her of a wild stallion that refused to be broken.

His eyes were deep brown, and the cold disdain in them was plain. It only made her want to tame him more.

Gavin's gaze swept over the glaring red marks on Evelyn's neck and the white silk tossed aside not far away. His brows furrowed in fresh disgust.

He wondered, 'What act is she putting on now? Has she called me here to play the victim and beg me to let her stay?'

Evelyn straightened up. "Gavin," she said softly.

Last night was their so-called wedding night. Gavin had spent it in his study with his strategy books, never once stepping into the bridal chamber.

That day at the Starlight Pavilion, Gavin's drink had been drugged with an aphrodisiac. The stuff had hit him like wildfire, leaving him dizzy and burning up. He'd stumbled into a private chamber to get air.

The room had been dark, with the curtains drawn. Fighting the heat, he'd moved toward the bed to rest, only to find a girl sleeping there. He'd barely gotten close when she gasped.

Then her maid had burst in, shouting "My lady!" and flung the door open.

In the sudden light, Gavin saw the girl's face. She looked pale, fragile, and scared. He also learned who she was: Evelyn, the heiress of the Sinclair family.

A man and a woman were alone in a private room, with him half-dressed. Even if nothing happened, her reputation was ruined because of him. So, he'd done the honorable thing and proposed.

But today, the truth came out. Evelyn wasn't the real heiress. Her own maid had even confessed that Evelyn had bribed someone at the Starlight Pavilion to drug his drink.

Gavin hated being manipulated. Her scheming disgusted him.

Now, Colelinton was buzzing with rumors that Evelyn was loose, that she'd been involved with many men. Whether they were true or not didn't matter. He wanted nothing more to do with her.

"Playing the victim won't work on me," Gavin said, his voice cold. "You're not staying here."

He thought, 'It doesn't matter if she was really trying to hurt herself or if it was just a show. The fact that she'd set me up is enough. No way I'm keeping her around.'

Evelyn arched a brow and strode right up to Gavin.

Aside from that day at the Starlight Pavilion, Gavin had never been this close to her. Now, he could see her face clearly.

She looked different. She'd done her makeup. Her brows were sharp, her eyes upturned at the corners with a hint of mischief. A faint red beauty mark near her eye added a mysterious charm. Her lashes were long, and her eyes sparkled.

Gavin couldn't help wondering, 'The helpless, delicate act is gone. Now that her mask is off, is this the real her?'

"Gavin..." Evelyn said, lifting her hand. Her fingertips nearly grazed his shoulder—a deliberate tease. When he frowned, she simply reached past him and gently closed the door. For a moment, their breaths mingled in the quiet room.

"Isn't it tiring to stand?" she asked, tilting her head. The beauty mark at the corner of her eye seemed to shimmer. "Why don't we sit and talk?"

Gavin gave her a long look, then walked over and sat in the chair. The moment he did, a sharp pain shot through the back of his neck.

A wave of numbness rushed down his spine, leaving his arms limp. The curved back of the chair caught his elbows as they went slack.

Before he could react, Evelyn yanked a red silk ribbon from the bed curtains. In a few swift moves, she tied his upper body to the chair. He was trapped.

Gavin never saw this coming. He snapped his head up, staring at her in disbelief. "You're tying me up?"

Evelyn had learned those pressure-point techniques from a master in her previous life, and they were finally useful.

Her fingers traced the tense lines of his chest and then began undoing the buttons of his robe. Once it was open, her hand drifted lower, to his belt.

It hit Gavin then: he was tied to the chair, back straight, shoulders forced back, chest bare, belt loosened. He looked like some gift waiting to be unwrapped in a cheap romance novel, and she was the one untying the bow.

No one had ever dared treat him like this. A bitter, disbelieving laugh escaped him, but it died in his throat as she climbed onto his lap, pressing herself against him.

Her fingertips lightly brushed his Adam's apple. Even when he glared at her with murderous intensity, she didn't flinch.

"So this is your plan?" Gavin's voice was icy, his chest tight with anger. "You think this will make me keep you?"

His mind raced. 'She's too naive if she thinks a silk ribbon can hold me. If she's trying to seduce me to change my mind, I'll only despise her more.'

Evelyn seemed unfazed, a faint smile on her lips. "Haven't you heard? The whole city says I'm shameless. Since that's my reputation, I might as well show you why."

She finished with his belt, her palm flat against his warm stomach, her breath hot by his ear. "Right now, we're still married. What happens between us is only natural.

"If I wait much longer, we won't be. So I should take my chance while I have it. A man like you doesn't come along often."

Every muscle in Gavin's body was tense, his gaze frigid. He'd see how far she was willing to take this charade.

Evelyn kept her word. She wrapped an arm around his neck, her hips moving against him in a slow, deliberate rhythm. The friction sent a jolt through him, sharp and undeniable.

Gavin's eyes narrowed with icy disdain. "You're insane," he said.

But even as he spoke, his body responded instinctively, a traitorous heat pooling low in his stomach.

'Typical of the golden boy. But no matter how frosty he pretends to be, at the end of the day, he's still just a man,' Evelyn mused.

Evelyn grinned. "You think this is crazy? Trust me, I can get even crazier."

Just then, Sophie's surprised voice came from outside the door. "Lady Wendy? What brings you here?"

..............................................................................
Outside the door, Wendy, Gavin's mother, demanded in a stern, angry voice, "Where is that wretch?"

Sophie explained, "General Hawthorne is inside the room talking with her."

"What?" Hearing Gavin was in the room, Wendy frowned and knocked sharply. "Gavin, are you in there?"

Evelyn suddenly smiled. She tilted Gavin's chin up with her fingertips, forcing him to meet her gaze. Her eyes gleamed in the low light, a seductive blush coloring her pale cheeks. Then, without warning, she kissed him.

Her lips moved against his with a boldness that stole his breath. For a heartbeat, shock held him frozen. Then a raw, unwelcome surge of desire crashed through him, hot and urgent. He gripped the arms of the chair until his knuckles turned white.

"Enough," Gavin said, his voice low and harsh. This situation had spiraled completely out of his control.

Wendy had no clue what was happening inside and knocked more urgently. "Gavin, why won't you answer? What could you possibly have left to say to a woman like her?"

"Not enough..." Evelyn murmured, bit her lower lip hard, and kept moving her body.

In the next moment, Gavin's forehead broke out in a thin layer of sweat. A shudder ran through him as her body shifted intentionally against his, the thin layers of fabric between them doing little to mask the contact. Still, he didn't push her away.

It sounded like someone was about to enter, so Evelyn buried her face in the crook of his neck. Her eyes were slightly red, and when she spoke, her voice was muffled, trembling just a little.

She said, "I drugged you not to save myself, but because I love you. If I hadn't schemed, how else could I ever have gotten this close to you?"

Gavin stiffened all of a sudden.

"Don't come in," he finally shouted toward the door, his voice hoarse and rough.

The room was bathed in a soft, hazy light. Gavin stared at Evelyn.

The crimson ribbon that had bound him had come loose and now wrapped around her slender wrist, stark against her skin. Her long hair tumbled over it in a messy, tempting cascade.

The sight was pure temptation. But Gavin was a soldier, hardened by years of war. His willpower was formidable.

His gaze turned cold. In the next instant, his fingers dug into her waist, pulling her closer as his body moved against hers in a rhythm that was both claiming and restrained.

She gasped against his neck, her body trembling. But even now, riding the edge of his own desire, he would not lose control.

And in that suspended moment of heated friction, he felt it—the undeniable, yielding resistance that confirmed everything. He froze, swallowing hard, his throat tight.

Thankfully, those outside couldn't hear clearly. The two of them were husband and wife in name, yet this felt like something forbidden.

"Gavin!" Wendy's voice rose sharply through the door. "You tell me not to enter? What exactly are you doing in there?"

Evelyn looked up at him, breathless, tears glistening on her lashes. "Gavin," she whispered, her voice feather-light and fragile, soaked in unmistakable hurt.

Gavin's expression darkened, his hand still firm on her waist. He could feel the frantic beat of her heart against his palm.

"I still have matters to settle with her," he said, his tone unnaturally calm. "I'll handle this. There's no need for you to worry, Mother."

Wendy stood outside, her brow deeply furrowed, utterly confused by Gavin's behavior. Finally, with a frustrated sigh, she turned and left.

Once her footsteps faded and silence returned, Gavin locked his eyes on Evelyn's face. "Did you mean what you said?"

He studied her. Her cheeks were still flushed from their movements, but her lips were pale from being bitten so hard. She looked like a flower battered by a storm, seemingly fragile enough to break, yet still holding her head high.

The rumors claimed she'd been with countless men. But the physical evidence of her innocence moments ago was more convincing than any words. Against his better judgment, Gavin found himself starting to believe her.

Tears welled in Evelyn's eyes. "Of course," she replied.

She explained, "Two years ago, when you returned victorious, I saw you from a distance in the street. You were on that magnificent horse, the enemy's banner on your spear, your armor gleaming so brightly in the sun it hurt to look.

"When you reined in and glanced at the crowd, my heart stopped. After that, I wished every day to see you again. But I was stuck inside the manor with no way to meet you.

"I skipped all my etiquette lessons at Peanolie Manor, hiding in the book vault to read military books, trying to understand you a little better."

She let out a small, frustrated sigh. "But I'm hopeless. I could never understand any of the knowledge."

She didn't sound like she was making it up. After all, everyone in Colelinton knew she was barely literate, let alone well-educated.

She looked up at him, a tear trembling on her lashes. "The day I heard you'd be at the Starlight Pavilion, I lost my head and did that stupid thing.

"I thought that if I could marry you, I'd be by your side every day. Sooner or later, you'd see how I truly felt.

"I know I did wrong and deceived you. If you still wish to send me away, I'll accept it. I won't fight you."

As she finished speaking, the tear finally fell, tracing a shining path down her pale cheek. She looked utterly heartbroken.

Gavin closed his eyes briefly and then opened them. "Get up," he said, his voice tight and strangely rough.

He had assumed her scheming was purely for survival. But if it was truly born of affection, and things had gone this far... he figured he could show some mercy. An amicable separation would be better for both their reputations than a scandalous divorce.

Evelyn obeyed, rising from his lap. But as she moved, an ornate hairpin slipped from her hair and clattered to the floor.

Gavin instinctively looked over and saw a couple of small, dark red pellets roll out from the hairpin's intricate setting.

Gavin's gaze hardened, his voice frosty. "What is this?"

Evelyn glanced down, cursing inwardly, 'Well, caught red-handed. Of all times, this stupid hairpin slips now.'

Gavin shot to his feet, scooped up one of the pellets, and crushed it between his fingers. He brought it to his nose. The moment the cloying, sweet scent hit him, his expression changed. He flung the powder away as if it were poison.

He sneered inwardly, 'That smell... It's the same faint sweetness I detected in my wine that day at the Starlight Pavilion. She drugged me again.

'No wonder I almost lost control moments ago. It must have been the scent from her hairpin, clouding my judgment.'

In an instant, everything became clear to him. Now that her true identity was exposed, if he cast her out, she would have nowhere to go.

So she had gone all in, hiding aphrodisiacs and throwing herself at him. Then she'd spun that heartfelt confession, blurring calculation with sincerity, all to manipulate his pity.

Gavin's face was like thunder. 'Whoever said she was a fool was dead wrong. She's dangerously clever,' he thought.

..............................................................................
Gavin's face was grim. After a long silence, he finally spoke, his voice low, "Evelyn, you really are something."

Evelyn saw the raw disgust and icy contempt in his eyes, and felt the almost tangible pressure of his anger.

She'd planned to get it done in one go. Whether Gavin divorced her was irrelevant.

What mattered was branding herself into his memory so deeply that every other woman, even the real Sinclair family heiress, would seem dull by comparison.

As for the little something in her hairpin... She'd just wanted insurance. She knew how men could be.

Of course, she'd just proven her fears unnecessary. She hadn't expected Gavin to have that much restraint.

Now, she'd crashed and burned spectacularly.

Evelyn bit her lip, the flush at her eyes deepening, putting on a convincingly wounded expression. "I just wanted our first time to be... memorable."

Gavin looked at her. 'Now she's playing the victim again? After lying to me again?' he wondered.

He didn't believe a word she said. Now, she was all lies in his eyes.

"I'll have the divorce papers sent to Peanolie Manor," Gavin said, turning his back on her, his tone cold and firm. "Pack your things and get out of Hawthorne Estate by tonight. You and I are finished."

Evelyn didn't say more.

She thought, 'Getting dumped? Honestly, that's perfect. I've lived the best life there was. The idea of being tied to one man forever is worse than being killed with my body left in a mass grave.'

*****

Gavin had Evelyn's belongings sent back to Peanolie Manor. At dusk, Evelyn covered the marks on her neck with makeup and walked out of Hawthorne Estate without a backward glance. Just past the gate, she heard hurried footsteps behind her.

She turned to see a girl, about fourteen or fifteen, running after her, clutching a small bundle. The girl met her gaze, blushed fiercely, and nearly tripped over her own feet as she tried to curtsy. "My... My lady."

Evelyn recognized her as Hazel from her inherited memories.

When the original Evelyn had left Peanolie Manor, she'd taken four maids: Lana, her head maid, Stella, the seamstress, Cora, who handled meals, and Hazel, the quiet laundry maid.

Lana had been Evelyn's most trusted maid since childhood, but was the first to betray her as soon as her identity came to light. Not only did Lana expose the drugging incident to Gavin, but she also led the other maids back to Peanolie Manor.

Evelyn never expected Hazel, the most overlooked of the four maids, to stay.

"Why are you still here?" Evelyn asked, noting Hazel's faded, threadbare dress.

"I'm your maid," Hazel said, her head bowed. "I go where you go. If you stay, I stay. If you return to Peanolie Manor, I follow."

Evelyn raised an eyebrow. "You know I'm not the real Sinclair family heiress."

Hazel bit her lip. "Then I still have to return and face Lord Richard and Lady Margaret's judgment. Until then, you're my mistress."

Evelyn's eyes fell on a faint scar by Hazel's temple, which was a mark from a hairbrush the original Evelyn had thrown in a rage. "I wasn't kind to you. Yet you remain loyal," Evelyn said.

Hazel was quiet for a moment and then looked up with tears in her eyes. "You just had a bad temper. But when my mom was dying, you let me go home to care for her. I wouldn't have gotten to say goodbye otherwise.

"A low-ranking maid at Peanolie Manor isn't allowed such things without permission."

Evelyn recalled the incident. The original Evelyn only sent Hazel away to stop her incessant crying. A gesture of annoyance was remembered as profound kindness by Hazel.

Looking at Hazel's sincere face, Evelyn thought of the fawning, silk-clad servants of her previous life. None had possessed Hazel's simple loyalty.

"Then come with me," Evelyn said. She thought, 'The real show is about to begin at Peanolie Manor.'

*****

In the main hall of Peanolie Manor, incense smoke curled from a bronze burner. An autumn breeze rustled past the eaves, scattering a few dry leaves.

Richard gripped the divorce papers from Hawthorne Estate, the veins on his hands standing out. His wife, Margaret, clutched a silk handkerchief, her face equally stormy.

"This is an utter disgrace!" Richard slammed the papers onto the table.

"First, we were exposed for having a fake daughter. Now, we're publicly humiliated by this divorce. The entire city is laughing at us for getting the wrong daughter and raising a shameless girl," he added, his chest heaving with anger.

Beside him and Margaret sat a young woman with red-rimmed eyes. She'd once been one of the lowest scullery maids in the manor, forced to empty chamber pots and scrub floors. She'd also been cruelly nicknamed "Plain Jane" by Evelyn.

Now, restored as the true heiress and renamed Rosalie Sinclair, she wore a fine new dress, a valuable emerald bracelet, and an elegant hairpin. Yet, a timid air still clung to her.

"Father, Mother, please don't upset yourselves over this," she said softly, her voice laced with concern.

Richard glanced at his daughter's nervous, hesitant demeanor, and a complex wave of emotion washed over him.

He and Margaret had never expected that the girl they'd cherished for sixteen years was a stranger's abandoned child.

And the bitter irony was that their true daughter had been under their roof all along, degraded to the lowest chores, bullied and abused by the impostor.

If Lana hadn't come back with the other maids today and laid bare all of Evelyn's sins—the bullying, the scheming, the drugging—they would still be blind, believing they'd raised a precious jewel.

The thought made Richard's temples pound. Staring at the divorce papers, he felt nothing but shame. The noble families who once curried favor with the Sinclair family now had the perfect scandal to feast upon.

"Father, Evelyn is vile. If she dares show her face here, we should throw her out!" The speaker was Edward Sinclair, Richard's second son.

At seventeen, he was tall and lean, his hair tied back with a loose red ribbon, a few stray strands falling across his forehead.

He had sharp features and bright eyes, but anger made him look like a provoked young animal, which was fierce yet still bearing traces of youthful immaturity.

His voice dripped with contempt when he mentioned Evelyn.

..............................................................................
Edward had never liked Evelyn. Other people's younger sisters were refined and accomplished, of whom some could recite poems or entire classical texts, some were skilled in detailed nature paintings, and others could discuss military history.

But Evelyn couldn't even write legibly. Once, at a salon, she'd confused "snow like cherry blossoms" with "cherry blossoms like snow cookies," sending the room into laughter and leaving Edward deeply embarrassed.

Only today did he learn that Evelyn wasn't even his real sister.

When he saw the scars on Rosalie's arm—round burns from incense ash, scabs from cane strikes, old and new marks layered over one another—a fire ignited in his chest.

Edward thought, 'How could someone be so vicious, abusing their power like this?

'A cast-off woman with no money has nowhere to go. She'll have to return to the manor. But I won't allow someone like her to come back.

'A person like her, who's done so much evil, deserves to be thrown out for good. I never want to see her again.'

Just then, a servant rushed in, panicked. "My lord, my lady, Lady—"

He was about to say "Lady Evelyn," but cut himself off when seeing Richard's and Margaret's dark expressions. He swallowed hard and continued, "The one who was thrown out of Hawthorne Estate... She's back."

*****

Outside the gates of Peanolie Manor, Lana leaned against a bronze lion, arms crossed. She'd predicted Evelyn would come crawling back like a stray dog, so she'd brought a few sturdy maids to wait.

The sun was scorching. Lana patted powder onto her palms. Hearing carriage wheels in the distance, she straightened up.

Spotting Evelyn, Lana lifted her chin. "Well, well, look who it is. The 'precious jewel' of the Sinclair family herself.

"Does she think she's still the noble young lady she used to be? How shameless she is to show her face here again."

The maids behind her tittered. One raised her voice. "Lana, look, her makeup's smudged. She must have cried all the way here."

"No surprise. One day, she's the cherished Lady Evelyn, and the next, she's a nobody, divorced on the very morning after her wedding. Of course, she'd cry," another jeered.

In fact, Evelyn's makeup was perfect, her brows neatly shaped, her lip color unsmeared.

In the past, Evelyn had treated these servants terribly. Now that she'd fallen from grace, they were eager to kick her while she was down, unleashing years of pent-up resentment, especially Lana.

As Evelyn's personal maid for years, except for Plain Jane, Lana had endured the most. For example, she had to kneel to massage Evelyn's legs every night, getting her palm jabbed with a hairpin if she pressed too hard.

Now the tables had turned. Everyone in the manor thought that Evelyn, the fake heiress with no real family, was beneath even the lowliest maids. Lana wasn't about to miss her chance to finally get even.

Hazel stood behind Evelyn, wanting to tell her to ignore them. But Evelyn's expression didn't change. She walked over, calm and steady, and slapped Lana hard across the face.

The sharp smack made Lana stagger back. Clutching her stinging cheek, she stared in disbelief. "You... you hit me?"

"Why wouldn't I?" Evelyn's gaze was icy. "My name is still in the family registry. You're just a servant who signed a contract. How dare you act so bold in front of your mistress?"

Tears welled in Lana's eyes, but she jutted her chin out defiantly. "You think you're still Lady Evelyn? You're nothing but—"

Evelyn slapped her again, knocking her to the ground. "Nothing but what?"

Evelyn leaned down, meeting Lana's frightened eyes, then slowly pulled a silk handkerchief from her sleeve and wiped her fingers. "As long as my name's in the registry, you will have to respect me. Understood?"

The other maids were stunned. They never thought that, after being exposed as a fake heiress and cast out by the Hawthorne family, Evelyn would still act with such audacity. The slaps had been fiercer than any she'd given before.

Evelyn smoothed a stray lock of hair and glanced at Hazel. "Come with me," she said, her voice steady, as if nothing had happened.

When word of the incident at the gates reached him, Richard was furious. "She's out of control! Bring her in here now."

As soon as he finished speaking, Evelyn walked in, every step measured. She offered a perfect curtsy. "Father. Mother."

The address made Richard and Margaret grimace as if they'd tasted something foul. The more they remembered doting on Evelyn, the more they hated her for the years of cruelty toward their real daughter.

Edward jumped to his feet. "Shut up! You're not our blood. Don't call them that again."

Evelyn looked up, feigning confusion. "Then what should I call them? Fake Father? Fake Mother?"

"You—" Edward's face flushed red. He was speechless.

"Enough!" Richard slammed his hand on the table.

He glared at Evelyn, chest heaving. "All these years, we treated you like a treasure. Who knew you were so vicious at heart?"

"Now that you know the truth, you should understand. There's no way we will keep you," Richard added. Anger made him cough violently.

Rosalie hurried over, her white sleeve brushing the table. She patted his back with a lily-embroidered handkerchief in her hand, eyes red and pitiful. "Father, are you all right?"

Evelyn's lazy gaze swept over Rosalie's expensive honey-colored brocade dress. A faint smile touched her lips. "So Plain Jane isn't so plain after all. You look quite nice in the same clothes as mine."

Rosalie stiffened, her fingers digging into her palms. 'How dare this evil woman still call me that? I'm now the real heiress of the Sinclair family. I never want to hear that name again,' she yelled inwardly.

Evelyn looked away and drew a piece of paper from her sleeve. "Before you throw me out, Father and Mother, perhaps you should look at this first."

Richard had no idea what she was up to. When the paper was handed to him, he and Margaret read the messy handwriting and nearly fainted.

..............................................................................
The unfolded paper listed a dozen or so "crimes."

[On March 27, in the 17th year of King Nathan's reign, Father met with Martin Thornton, the Crown Prince's subordinate, at the Prime Inn in the west of the city to discuss forming a political alliance.]

[On November 5, in the 19th year of King Nathan's reign, Father secretly visited a weapons workshop on the outskirts to meet with craftsmen.]

[On August 14, in the 20th year of King Nathan's reign, Father spoke recklessly at home after drinking, insulting His Majesty King Nathan.]

[On April 9, in the 22nd year of King Nathan's reign, Father harbored a wanted fugitive and aided his escape.]

The rest on the paper had similar content.

As they read the list of "crimes," Richard and Margaret were so mad that they were seeing stars.

Each charge was circled in red, like a death sentence from the Supreme Court. Any one of these reaching the king could mean execution and the end of their family.

"What the hell is this? I've never done any of these things." Richard's eyes widened in shock, and he glared at Evelyn in disbelief.

"You're right. You didn't do any because I made them up," Evelyn said calmly. "But if these accusations came from me and reached His Majesty, even if he doubted them, his trust in the Sinclair family would be shaken.

"Besides, the part about you complaining about His Majesty after drinking... It's true. Given His Majesty's suspicious nature, he would be furious."

As the daughter raised in the manor, Evelyn knew all the family's dirty secrets.

If the Sinclair family cast her out heartlessly, it was only natural she'd reveal everything out of spite. To any outsider, her story would sound plausible.

She thought, 'Only the liar knows how innocent their target truly is.'

Richard's eyes were fixed on Evelyn. It hit him then: the girl, once so familiar to him, seemed like a stranger now. She looked sweet and innocent, but it felt as if a mask had slipped, revealing a demon's true form.

His jaw tightened, and he asked through gritted teeth, "What do you want?"

"Not much. Just announce publicly that you're taking me in as your adopted daughter, and have everyone continue to call me Lady Evelyn." Evelyn tilted her head, a sweet smile curling her lips. "Do that, and I won't spread any rumors."

"And I'll be sensible," she added, idly smoothing her dress. "I'll take the empty Grove House in the West Wing. Rosalie can have my old place, Radiance House. I'll keep only Hazel with me. I won't trouble the rest of the staff."

She looked up suddenly, a teasing glint in her eye. "You're not thinking about silencing me, are you, Father?"

Without waiting for an answer, she chuckled softly. "I'm sure you wouldn't be so ruthless after raising me all these years. Besides, if I was bold enough to say all this, I've naturally taken precautions."

Richard felt his blood boil. The girl they'd raised wasn't just an ungrateful soul. She was a cunning, ruthless predator. He'd meant to cast her out, but was facing her threat now. For the moment, she was untouchable.

Seeing his reaction, Evelyn smiled and curtsied again, her voice soft as a breeze. "Then, Father, Mother, I shall take my leave."

*****

At Peanolie Manor, the East Wing signified status, the West Wing disgrace. The West Wing's tiles were mossy, weeds growing by the walls. It was where lesser important family members and the servants lived, a place the old Evelyn would never have set foot in.

Evelyn chose it for the peace and quiet.

Grove House had been intended for a new concubine but had stood locked and empty for years. Cobwebs hung from the eaves.

Inside, plants in the courtyard leaned haphazardly, the ground covered in dead leaves. Broken window paper fluttered, revealing dusty furniture and mold-spotted curtains in the rooms.

Evelyn had never lived anywhere so run-down. But according to the original story, she should be dead by now. 'It's fine. I'll fix it up once I have money,' she thought.

Hazel, knowing Evelyn was unused to such conditions, hastily promised to clean up while Evelyn waited outside. She dragged a dusty sandalwood chair from the storage room and placed it under a tree.

Evelyn glanced at the shabby chair, unable to hide her disgust.

Hazel frantically wiped it with her sleeve until the wood shone, and laid a clean handkerchief over the seat. Only then did Evelyn reluctantly sit down.

Just then, a cool, mocking voice came from behind. "You're living in the West Wing now, and you're still putting on airs?"

Evelyn turned around and spotted a skinny young guy standing in the dappled shade.

His long hair was messy, falling over half his face. His skin was pale, as if he seldom saw the sun. He was strikingly handsome, but a sarcastic smirk played on his lips.

From the shadows, his gaze burned with a dark intensity. An air of gloom clung to him.

Evelyn recognized him as Julian Sinclair. In the manor, his name was rarely spoken with anything but a sneer, even by many of the servants. That same air of disregard clung to the boy himself, wrapped in a gloom that seemed bred in the shadows.

As a concubine's son, he was two months younger than Evelyn. His mother, Zayla, had been one of Margaret's scullery maids. Zayla was only with Richard because he got drunk one night.

Ten years ago, Zayla was accused of disrespecting Margaret and sold off to a remote rural estate.

It was said Zayla had been caught cursing Margaret in front of her mirror, and a hex doll stuck with pins was found hidden under her pillow, discovered by Margaret's head maid.

All these years, Julian had lived in the manor, ignored by Richard and everyone else.

But Evelyn had seen enough palace schemes. She could tell from the memories she had inherited that this was nothing but a classic frame-up.

Margaret would never tolerate a scullery maid rising in status because of a child. In her eyes, a lowly servant with a pretty face seduced her drunk husband and had a child, and that was an unforgivable sin.

Zayla's "disrespect" was just Margaret's excuse to remove a thorn in her side. As for the whole "seduction while drunk" story, Evelyn knew that if someone was really drunk, they'd just be unconscious.

Evelyn thought, 'Being drunk is just the excuse men use for dirty deeds. They twist it around, put the blame on the woman, and claim she was "trying to climb up by seduction." Richard is a real piece of garbage.'

The old Evelyn had been too naive to see through any of this.

After Zayla was sold, every glimpse of Julian reminded her of the supposed curses. Whenever she passed the West Wing corridor and spotted him, she would press a handkerchief to her nose and let out a cold laugh.

She would either hurl coffee cups at his feet or order her maids to splash him with dirty water, finding endless ways to humiliate her "servant-born" half-brother.

Evelyn thought, 'Now, the tables have turned. My current standing is arguably worse than his.'

She looked at Julian. She alone knew the truth: Zayla, the lowly maid, was actually the only daughter of the head of the wealthy Sherman family in Southerbone, kidnapped as a child and sold into servitude.

Zayla had died years ago, but her father, Frank, had never stopped searching. He'd eventually trace the clues to Peanolie Manor, hoping to find his long-lost grandson.

In the original story, Evelyn had tormented Julian, while Rosalie's kindness had been his light. Later, the vast fortune he inherited from Frank would be willingly placed at Rosalie's feet for her to use as she pleased.

'Well, talk about luck,' Evelyn thought, her spirits lifting. 'Just when I need money, a future goldmine of a little brother drops right into my lap.'
